1: koh her'ani Adonai YHWH — "This is what the Lord YHWH showed me." The vision report formula (koh her'ani) marks the transition to the five-vision cycle (7:1-9:4). The first vision: locusts forming at the beginning of the late crop — the leqesh (late planting) after the king's first mowing. The timing is critical: the king's portion has been taken, leaving only the late crop to sustain the population. The locusts threaten total consumption of what little remains.
2: va-yhi im killa le-echol et esev ha-aretz — "when they had finished eating the grass of the land." Amos intercedes: "O Lord YHWH, please forgive — how can Jacob stand? He is so small!" (ki qaton hu). The intercession acknowledges Israel's vulnerability and smallness — not its righteousness. The appeal is to divine mercy, not covenant merit. YHWH relents (nacham, a divine change of disposition in response to intercession): "It shall not be."
3: va-yinachem YHWH al-zot — "YHWH relented concerning this." Divine relenting (nacham niphal) is a recurring motif in the prophets — a genuine responsiveness of YHWH to prophetic intercession. The decree is provisional, not absolute; it can be withheld when a mediator stands in the breach. The first vision ends with the judgment averted. The repetition of this pattern in v. 6 makes the third vision's lack of relenting (v. 8) all the more decisive.
4: koh her'ani Adonai YHWH — Second vision: fire called by YHWH for judgment, consuming the great deep (tehom rabbah) and devouring the land (haheleq, possibly the agricultural allotment). The scope is cosmic — the subterranean waters that sustain the earth are consumed. Again Amos intercedes with the same formula: "O Lord YHWH, please stop — how can Jacob stand? He is so small!"
5: YHWH Elohim sh'lach-na — "O Lord YHWH, please stop." The second intercession uses a different verb: chadal (cease, stop) rather than s'lach (forgive) from v. 2. The slight variation may reflect escalation — the second vision's destruction is cosmic in scale, and the request is simply for cessation. YHWH again relents: "This also shall not be."
6: nacham YHWH al-zot — "YHWH relented concerning this." The second relenting mirrors the first. Two intercessions, two retractions. The pattern makes the third vision structurally climactic — when YHWH holds his ground and refuses to relent (v. 8), the reader understands that a threshold has been crossed.
7: ve-hineh Adonai nitzav al-chomat anakh — "the Lord was standing beside a wall built true to plumb, with a plumb line (anakh) in his hand." Anakh is likely an Akkadian loanword (anakku = lead or tin) — its rarity lends the vision particularity. The wall has been built true; YHWH is measuring with the same standard Israel was built to. The plumb line is not an instrument of construction now but of judgment — measuring existing reality against the original standard.
8: mah atah ro'eh Amos — "What do you see, Amos?" The divine question elicits the prophetic report. YHWH declares: "I am setting a plumb line (anakh) in the midst of my people Israel. I will never again pass over them (lo osif od avor lo)." The phrase "pass over" (avor) may carry the resonance of the Exodus passover — YHWH who once passed over Israel to protect them will now pass through for judgment. The high places of Isaac and the sanctuaries of Israel will be desolated.
9: ve-qamti al beit Yaravam b'cherev — "I will rise against the house of Jeroboam with the sword." The plumb line vision concludes with a specific dynastic threat against Jeroboam II. This specific political prediction is what triggers Amaziah's report to Jeroboam and his confrontation with Amos in the following section. The connection between visionary oracle and political reaction is direct.
10: va-yishlach Amazyahu kohen Beit-El el-Yaravam — "Amaziah, the priest of Bethel, sent to Jeroboam." Amaziah is the royal priest of the state sanctuary at Bethel — Jeroboam I's anti-Jerusalem alternative to Temple worship. His accusation: Amos has conspired (qashar) against Jeroboam in the midst of the house of Israel. The charge of conspiracy is political, transforming prophetic proclamation into sedition. "The land is not able to bear all his words" — lo tuchal ha-aretz le-hacil et kol devarav — the prophetic speech is too heavy for the political order to sustain.
11: ki koh amar Amos — "for thus Amos said." Amaziah quotes — and slightly misquotes — Amos's message: "Jeroboam shall die by the sword, and Israel must go into exile away from its land." The reported speech may compress several oracles. Whether accurate or not, the summary captures the essence of Amos's threat, which is the real reason for expulsion.
12: va-yomer Amazyahu el-Amos — "Amaziah said to Amos." The command to leave has economic and territorial rationale: "Go to the land of Judah — eat bread there and prophesy there." "Eat bread there" implies that prophesying was a paid profession — Amaziah assumes Amos is a professional prophet pursuing his trade in the wrong market. The royal sanctuary at Bethel belongs to the crown and has no room for unauthorized proclamation.
13: u-v'Beit-El lo tosif od le-hinnavei — "at Bethel you shall not prophesy again." The finality of the prohibition frames the encounter as an institutional suppression of prophetic speech. Bethel is "the king's sanctuary and a royal house (beit mamlakhah)" — state religion explicitly serving the political order. The alignment of sanctuary with palace is the structural problem Amos's entire ministry exposes.
14: lo navi anochi — "I am not a prophet." Amos's self-identification is one of the most debated in the OT. He disclaims being a navi (prophet) or a ben navi (son of a prophet, member of prophetic guild). He was a boqer (herdsman; the word may denote a specific type of cattle-raiser) and a boles shiqmim (dresser of sycamore figs — one who nicks the fig to accelerate ripening, a task associated with the poor). The disclaimer distinguishes Amos from professional prophets who prophesy for hire.
15: va-yiqacheni YHWH me-achar ha-tzon — "YHWH took me from following the flock." Amos's authority derives entirely from divine calling, not professional standing. The verb laqach (take) is the same used for the charismatic seizure of prophets and leaders throughout the OT — it is the act of divine appropriation that creates a messenger. The legitimacy of his prophetic speech rests on the One who sent him, not on any institutional credential.
16: atah shema devar YHWH — "Now hear the word of YHWH." Turning Amaziah's expulsion command back, Amos delivers a specific judgment oracle against the priest himself. The oracle addresses what Amaziah tried to silence — and silencing is precisely the transgression that triggers the personal judgment.
17: ishtekha ba-ir tizneh — "Your wife shall become a prostitute in the city." The judgment on Amaziah mirrors his institutional sin: he tried to silence the covenant voice at the royal sanctuary; his judgment includes the desecration of everything he protected — his wife dishonored, his children killed, his land divided by survey line (be-chevel yechulag), himself dying in a foreign unclean land. The priest of Israel's most prominent sanctuary will die in exile on impure soil. "Israel shall surely go into exile away from its land" — Amaziah's personal judgment is the microcosm of the national judgment.
